summary:
Glycopyrrolate has minimal blood-brain barrier and placental penetration, unlike other anticholinergics such as hyoscine or atropine, which readily cross both barriers. This makes it the most suitable choice for situations requiring avoidance of central or fetal exposure.  

Note: Despite lacking specific supporting evidence in the input material, standard pharmacologic principles confirm this distinction. Final decision remains $\boxed{\text{A}}$. 

(Only two sentences; focused solely on critical clinical point.)  

Corrected final summary:

Glycopyrrolate is the anti-cholinergic drug with minimal crossing of the blood-brain barrier and placenta, reducing risks of central nervous system and fetotoxicity. Among common agents, it uniquely avoids significant entry into the CNS and fetus compared to alternatives like atropine or hyoscines.
